page: Microsoft Azure D128ns_v6 is an x86_64 architecture server instance belonging to the Dnv6 server family. It features 128 dedicated vCPUs powered by Intel processors and is managed via the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. The instance is equipped with 512.0 GB of system memory, providing a balanced allocation of 4.0 GB of RAM per virtual core.

This server type does not include any local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met using external network-attached storage. It is designed with Premium Storage capabilities to support high-throughput remote disk configurations. The Dnv6 family is designated as network-optimized, prioritizing network throughput and interface density over local storage resources.

From a resource density perspective, the D128ns_v6 offers a high-capacity, single-node environment for workloads requiring substantial parallel processing without the overhead of local disk maintenance. The primary tradeoff is the complete reliance on remote storage, which introduces network latency considerations for disk I/O compared to instances with local NVMe drives. The lack of integrated GPUs also limits its utility for hardware-accelerated graphics or deep learning workloads.

The balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio makes this instance suitable for large-scale general-purpose applications, network-heavy middle-tier services, distributed caching, and enterprise database deployments that utilize premium remote storage.
description: Microsoft Azure D128ns_v6 is an x86_64 architecture server instance within the network-optimized Dnv6 family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 128 dedicated vCPUs powered by Intel processors and 512.0 GB of system memory, establishing a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core ratio. This instance does not include local storage, relying entirely on remote Premium Storage options for disk operations. Designed for high network throughput, the D128ns_v6 is structured for workloads that demand significant parallel compute capacity and high network bandwidth without requiring local physical disks or GPU acceleration. The primary tradeoff is the reliance on network-attached storage, which makes it suitable for enterprise application servers, large-scale network appliances, and distributed database nodes that leverage external premium storage volumes.
